event: Hunter-gatherers first reach the larger Solomon Islands during the Pleistocene, settling islands such as Buka and Guadalcanal via short inter-island sea crossings.
Papuan-speaking peoples expand their presence across the main Solomon archipelago, developing horticulture and coastal settlement.
Austronesian-speaking voyagers associated with the Lapita cultural complex arrive, introducing distinctive dentate-stamped pottery, outrigger canoes, and intensified agriculture.
Lapita settlers use the Solomon Islands as a staging point before voyaging onward to colonize Vanuatu, New Caledonia, Fiji, and West Polynesia.
Polynesian outlier communities, including settlers of Rennell, Bellona, Tikopia, and Ontong Java, establish distinct cultures within the archipelago.
Spanish navigator Alvaro de Mendana de Neira reaches the islands from Peru, names them the Islas Salomon in hopes of the biblical King Solomon's wealth, and explores Santa Isabel and Guadalcanal.
Mendana returns on a second expedition and discovers the Santa Cruz Islands, attempting a short-lived colony before dying at Santa Cruz.
Pedro Fernandes de Queiros, sailing on Mendana's earlier voyage, leads a Spanish expedition through the region en route to the New Hebrides.
British navigator Philip Carteret rediscovers parts of the islands, including Santa Cruz, after nearly two centuries of European neglect.
French explorer Louis-Antoine de Bougainville charts islands of the northern Solomons, lending his name to Bougainville Island.
British captain John Shortland sails through the islands, confirming and mapping the archipelago for European charts.
Whalers, traders, and beachcombers increasingly visit the islands, introducing steel tools, firearms, and new diseases to local communities.
Catholic Marist missionaries attempt an early mission on Santa Isabel, which ends in failure amid disease and conflict.
The labour trade known as blackbirding begins drawing Solomon Islanders, often by deception or force, to plantations in Queensland and Fiji.
Anglican Bishop John Coleridge Patteson is killed at Nukapu in the Santa Cruz Islands, an act linked to resentment over blackbirding abuses.
Britain declares a protectorate over the southern Solomon Islands, establishing the British Solomon Islands Protectorate.
Charles Morris Woodford is appointed the first Resident Commissioner, beginning effective British administration based at Tulagi.
Under the Tripartite Convention, Germany cedes Santa Isabel, Choiseul, the Shortlands, and Ontong Java to Britain in exchange for concessions over Samoa.
Tulagi is established as the colonial capital of the protectorate administration.
Lever's Pacific Plantations and other firms expand large coconut plantations, making copra the colony's economic mainstay.
The killing of district officer William Bell during a tax-collection patrol on Malaita, known as the Kwaio massacre, triggers a violent punitive expedition.
Japanese forces invade and occupy parts of the islands, and Allied troops land on Guadalcanal in August, opening one of the Pacific War's pivotal campaigns.
Allied forces secure Guadalcanal after fierce land, sea, and air battles, and future US president John F. Kennedy's PT-109 is sunk in Solomon waters.
Allied advances push Japanese forces out of the Solomons, leaving vast quantities of war wreckage that later draw divers to Iron Bottom Sound.
The Maasina Ruru (Marching Rule) movement emerges on Malaita, demanding self-government, local autonomy, and economic reform.
The protectorate capital is moved from Tulagi to Honiara on Guadalcanal, built around former wartime infrastructure.
A Legislative Council and an Executive Council are established, marking early steps toward representative self-government.
A new constitution introduces ministerial government, and Solomon Mamaloni becomes the first Chief Minister.
The islands achieve full internal self-government as a step toward independence from Britain.
Solomon Islands gains full independence from the United Kingdom on 7 July, with Peter Kenilorea as the first Prime Minister, and joins the United Nations.
The country becomes a member of the Commonwealth, retaining the British monarch as head of state represented by a Governor-General.
Cyclone Namu devastates the islands, killing around 100 people and causing widespread destruction to crops and infrastructure.
Ethnic tensions erupt on Guadalcanal as militants of the Isatabu Freedom Movement begin driving Malaitan settlers from rural areas.
Rival militias clash and the Malaita Eagle Force seizes weapons and forces Prime Minister Bartholomew Ulufa'alu from office in a coup, deepening the period known as the Tensions.
The Townsville Peace Agreement is signed to halt fighting between Guadalcanal and Malaitan militant groups.
At the government's request, the Australian-led Regional Assistance Mission to Solomon Islands (RAMSI) deploys to restore order, disarm militants, and rebuild institutions.
Rioting erupts in Honiara after a contested prime ministerial election, with much of the Chinatown district burned.
A powerful earthquake and tsunami strike the western islands, killing dozens and displacing thousands of people.
An earthquake and tsunami hit the Santa Cruz Islands in the east, causing fatalities and destruction in Temotu Province.
Flash floods devastate Honiara and Guadalcanal, killing more than 20 people and leaving thousands homeless.
Researchers report that five low-lying reef islands have disappeared and others severely eroded, documenting sea-level rise impacts in the Solomons.
RAMSI formally concludes its 14-year mission, transferring full security responsibility back to Solomon Islands authorities.
The government switches diplomatic recognition from Taiwan to the People's Republic of China, sparking domestic controversy.
Anti-government riots break out in Honiara over the China policy and provincial grievances, again destroying parts of Chinatown.
Solomon Islands signs a security pact with China, raising regional concern among Australia, the United States, and Pacific partners.
Honiara hosts the Pacific Games, the country's largest international sporting event, with venues built through Chinese assistance.
National elections are held and Jeremiah Manele becomes Prime Minister, succeeding Manasseh Sogavare amid coalition negotiations.
